upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very shallow mixed layer with a strong subsidence inversion near 850 mb (~1500 m / ~4900 ft MSL), evidenced by the dramatic temperature jump from 11.8°C at the surface to 14.6°C at 1483 m — a pronounced low-level inversion that will cap thermals well below ridge height for inland sites. The lapse rate from surface to 850 mb is only -1.89°C/km, far below dry adiabatic, confirming extremely stable air and very limited thermal development today. Upper winds at 700 mb are 11 kts from 250° and 22 kts at 500 mb from 280°, indicating light-to-moderate flow aloft with no significant shear hazard.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the SF Bay Area region. METARs paint a marine layer picture: KHAF reporting OVC016 with 320-340° winds at 6-8 kts (gust 12 at 1415Z), KAPC OVC015 with W-WNW 10-13 kts, KSFO FEW009 BKN017 with 26-30° at 6-9 kts, and KWVI OVC008 calm — indicating a well-established marine layer sitting at approximately 1500 ft across the Bay Area. KRHV is the exception: SKC with 33005KT, confirming clear skies in the Milpitas/south bay valley though winds are light. The marine stratus deck is thick (OVC at 800-1600 ft at coastal/bay stations) and the shallow inversion strongly limits burn-off potential.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland thermal/ridge site, NW-facing launch at 1750 ft
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's high launch works on a combination of thermally-driven valley breeze and afternoon sea breeze. Ideal conditions are NW flow 8-15 kts at launch with thermals cycling up from the valley. The 1750 ft launch sits above the marine layer on clear days but is sensitive to thermal strength and sea breeze timing.
sites.todaySetup: The deciding factor today is the extremely strong low-level inversion capping the BL at 300-335 m (~1000-1100 ft AGL above the valley floor), which effectively kills usable thermal development from the high launch. KRHV shows SKC with only 5 kts NNW — confirming the valley is clear but wind is light. BL heights from both GFS and HRRR agree: only 300-335 m during peak afternoon hours, far too shallow for meaningful thermal lift from the 1750 ft launch. Surface winds build to 7-8 kts WNW by 14-16Z, which is acceptable for ridge soaring, but with the inversion below launch height, pilots would essentially be flying in the capped stable layer with no thermal support. Tomorrow.io shows gusts only reaching 6-10 kts at this elevation with light mean winds (2-4 kts), suggesting punchy but weak thermals. The BL actually tops out at 335 m at 16Z — well below the 533 m (~1750 ft) launch elevation, meaning the launch sits above the thermal mixing layer entirely this afternoon.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light 1-4 kt WNW winds, BL only 80-325 m. No thermal support, winds too light for ridge lift. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 4-7 kts WNW by 13Z, BL peaks at ~330 m — still below launch elevation. Marginal ridge soaring possible but no thermal development to work with.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best surface winds of the day at 7-8 kts WNW (14-16Z), but BL remains capped at 310-335 m and clouds building (59-85% by 17-18Z). Window closes as cloud cover increases.

verdict.hazards: BL capped well below 1750 ft launch — launch sits above mixing layer. No thermal support. Increasing cloud cover PM. Light winds make sustained flight unlikely.

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill launch at ~600 ft, NW-facing
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch at Ed Levin is a gentler ridge/training site that benefits from the valley breeze and mild thermals off the lower slopes. It needs NW-NNW flow at 8-15 kts and works best when the BL is climbing through 200-400 m.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch is actually within reach of today's capped boundary layer, and this is the more viable Ed Levin option today. KRHV's SKC conditions and light NNW winds confirm clear air in the valley. BL heights build to 305-365 m by midday through early afternoon — just barely reaching above the 600 ft (~183 m) launch and supporting weak-to-moderate thermals near the lower slopes. Winds at the 600 ft site build to 8-9 kts WNW by 13-15Z per both GFS and HRRR, with Tomorrow.io gusts peaking at 7-10 kts — workable but not punchy. The catch is that the inversion remains firm; thermals will top out around 300-350 m AGL, making for shallow, scratchy soaring. Cloud cover increases significantly after 17Z (54-82%), narrowing the usable window to the midday-early afternoon period.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 5-6 kt NW winds, BL 155-305 m. Marginal conditions — light wind, thermals just beginning to develop off the lower slopes. Not recommended.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: 8-9 kts WNW, BL 345-365 m, clear skies (1-5% cloud). Weak thermals cycling up from the valley. Soarable for experienced pilots but capped low.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds steady 8-9 kts through 16Z then slight fade. Cloud cover surges to 54-82% by 17-18Z, killing thermals. Window closes by ~16-17Z.

verdict.hazards: Shallow thermal ceiling ~300-350 m AGL due to strong inversion. Cloud buildup after 3 PM ends flying. Low-altitude environment leaves little margin for error.

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal ridge, W-facing, sea breeze/ridge lift site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a W-facing coastal ridge that relies on steady onshore sea breeze and marine layer dynamics for ridge lift. Ideal winds are W-WNW at 10-20 kts. The site is heavily influenced by the marine layer — if the deck is below the ridge, expect limited visibility and rotor risk; if above, clean ridge soaring is possible.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock has the most consistent wind energy of any site today, but the marine layer is the story. KHAF is reporting OVC016 (1600 ft ceiling) with 320-340° winds at 6-8 kts, and buoy 46012 (Half Moon Bay) shows SST of 13.8°C, wave period of 11s, and 9.7 kts from 310° — a cool, well-established marine layer that is NOT going to burn off significantly today given the strong low-level inversion at ~1500 m. GFS and HRRR are in full agreement: W-WNW flow at 9-12 kts building through the day, with 80 m BL wind at 11-15 kts. Tomorrow.io shows consistent 8-10 kts W-WNW with gusts to 12 kts — solidly in the good ridge soaring range. The ceiling at KHAF (OVC016 = ~490 m AGL at the coast) is actually above the Mussel Rock ridge top (~150 m), so flying below the deck is feasible. Winds are clean and steady from WNW — textbook Mussel Rock direction. The 100% cloud cover noted in GFS during morning hours is the marine stratus, but it should thin somewhat at the ridge level by afternoon per model data showing cloud pct dropping to 19-24% by 12-16Z.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Marine stratus mostly overcast (78-100% cloud), but winds already 8-9 kts W-WNW. Flyable for locals comfortable in muted light, but ceiling low at ~490 m — stay below.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: marine layer thins to 19-29% cloud cover, winds 10-11 kts W-WNW with 80 m winds at 12-13 kts. Solid ridge lift, good visibility. Prime time.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds maintain 10-12 kts WNW through 19Z, though marine stratus returns (88-90% cloud by 17-19Z). Tomorrow.io shows gusts up to 12 kts — conditions remain soarable but visibility may tighten with thickening overcast.

verdict.hazards: Marine stratus deck — low ceilings possible morning and late afternoon (OVC016 at KHAF). Gusts to 12 kts (Tomorrow.io). Rotor risk below the ridge if winds exceed 18 kts — watch the building afternoon flow.

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland thermal/ridge site, SW-facing, 2517 ft summit
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak is a thermally active NW-facing ridge above the Fremont/Milpitas valley. It needs NW-WNW flow at 10-18 kts and working thermals to provide a soarable day. The site can be punchy when the sea breeze accelerates through the Altamont corridor.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares the same sounding and inland airmass as Ed Levin, and the same brutal inversion problem applies — BL heights only reaching 300-335 m AGL during peak hours, far below the ~767 m summit (2517 ft). The KRHV SKC/light-wind surface picture confirms the valley is thermally quiet. GFS and HRRR agree on WNW winds building to 7-8 kts by 14-15Z, which is within range, but without usable thermal development the ridge would require sustained mechanical/ridge lift alone. Tomorrow.io shows significant disagreement on wind direction at Mission Peak — showing WSW 250-270° at 4-5 kts with gusts to 7-10 kts, and critically, cloud cover at 77-87% with visibility only 3-4 km at 14-16Z. This cloudiness and poor visibility at peak level likely reflects the marine stratus wrapping around from the bay side — not a clean flyable window. The combination of capped thermals, marginal wind, poor visibility, and cloud cover makes this a no-go.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light 1-4 kt WNW, BL 80-325 m, clear. No lift, winds too light. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 6-7 kts WNW per GFS/HRRR, but Tomorrow.io shows 4-5 kts WSW with 77-87% cloud cover and visibility 3-4 km at peak. Conflicting data points to marginal-to-poor conditions; cloud wrap a serious concern.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 7-8 kts per models but cloud cover lingers 59-85% through 18Z per GFS. Tomorrow.io shows visibility improving slightly after 18Z but conditions remain compromised. No reliable soaring window.

verdict.hazards: BL capped well below summit elevation. Cloud wrap and poor visibility (3-4 km) at peak level per Tomorrow.io. Inversion kills thermal development. Not a safe or productive flying day.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Wednesday April 8 looks more promising. GFS/HRRR show BL heights climbing to 370-425 m by midday-afternoon, finally approaching the 533 m launch elevation. Winds build to 8-9 kts WNW at 10m, with 80m winds reaching 10 kts. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 15 kts at 08-10Z (possibly too strong early) moderating to 8-12 kts by afternoon. Marginal to flyable, pending morning marine layer clearing — 92% cloud cover at 08Z per GFS needs to burn off. Best window likely 12-16Z.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Better day at the 600 ft hill Wednesday. BL heights to 370-425 m, winds 7-9 kts WNW. Tomorrow.io gusts to 12 kts. Morning cloud cover (OVC equivalent) should clear by 10Z. Good soaring window 10 AM – 4 PM with workable thermals. Cleaner conditions than today.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Wednesday looks solid again. GFS/HRRR show W-WNW winds 8-10 kts building through the day, with 80m winds at 9-13 kts. Tomorrow.io gusts 10-14 kts. Morning marine layer likely (33% cloud at 08Z clearing quickly), then clean ridge soaring 10 AM through late afternoon. Watch the 18Z Tomorrow.io gust of 14.8 kts — could get sporty into the evening.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Similar to Ed Levin 1750 ft — BL heights improving to 350-395 m but still marginal below the 767 m summit. Winds 7-9 kts WNW. Tomorrow.io shows morning cloud/fog issue again at the peak (100% cloud, low visibility at 14Z) which is concerning. Afternoon window possible if clearing occurs but high uncertainty. Marginal at best.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Thursday April 9 shows a significant pattern change — winds shift southerly (166-270°), CAPE values spike to 120-320 J/kg, 100% cloud cover all day, and BL heights briefly surge to 1390 m by 14Z but with 16% precip probability. This is a pre-frontal or frontal passage signature. NOT FLY — active weather, unstable air, precipitation possible.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Same frontal pattern as the 1750 ft launch. High CAPE (120-320 J/kg), S/SW winds, 100% cloud, precip risk. No fly Thursday.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Thursday shows a complete wind direction reversal to SSW (207-252°), with 100% cloud cover, CAPE values 110-120 J/kg, and precip probability 9-13%. This is a frontal system — onshore flow from the wrong direction, unstable air, rain likely. No fly.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Same frontal pattern — SSW winds, 100% cloud, high CAPE (310 J/kg PM), precip risk 15%. BL heights briefly reach 1420 m but under fully overcast unstable conditions. No fly Thursday.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 (Friday April 10) not yet in the provided GFS/HRRR window. Based on the Thursday frontal passage, post-frontal clearing and a return to NW flow is possible Friday, potentially resetting for a better weekend. Monitor closely as the pattern evolves.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Same post-frontal recovery scenario as the 1750 ft. If the front clears Thursday night, Friday could see improving NW flow and good thermal conditions. Data not yet available — check updated forecast.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Coastal sites often recover quickly after frontal passages with strong NW flow. Friday could be excellent at Mussel Rock if post-frontal clearing arrives as expected. Watch the gradient — post-frontal NW acceleration can bring strong flow.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Post-frontal Friday TBD. Frontal passages can leave residual moisture and cloud at inland peaks. NW flow likely returns but thermal quality will depend on how quickly the airmass stabilizes behind the front.

watchlist: Marine stratus deck well-established with KHAF OVC016 all morning — coastal sites need this to thin for good visibility; it may thicken again late afternoon
Strong low-level inversion at ~1500 m (850 mb) caps BL at only 300-335 m AGL — kills thermal flying at high inland launches today

Tomorrow.io and GFS disagree on Mission Peak afternoon cloud cover and visibility — Tomorrow.io showing 77-87% cloud and 3-4 km vis at peak while GFS shows clearing; weight the worse scenario for safety
Thursday April 9 shows frontal passage signature: S/SW winds, high CAPE 120-320 J/kg, 100% overcast, precip probability 13-16% — all Bay Area sites are NO FLY
Buoy 46013 Bodega Bay showing 15s wave period with 13.6 kts NW — long-period swell indicates active NW swell, may increase marine layer persistence at coastal sites
Tomorrow.io gusts at Ed Levin 600 ft reaching 9-10 kts Wednesday morning (15-16 kts at 1750 ft) — early arrivals Wednesday should wait for winds to moderate before launching
tldr: Mussel Rock is your go-to today — steady 10-12 kts WNW with the marine layer thinning midday for a clean ridge soaring window from about 11 AM to 3 PM. Both Ed Levin launches suffer from a brutal low-level inversion capping the BL well below useful thermal heights, and Mission Peak gets cloud wrap with poor visibility. Heads up: a frontal system moves through Thursday making all sites a no-fly, so get your reps in today and Wednesday.
